content.text: What a DPIA Is and Why Claude Deployments Trigger It

A Data Protection Impact Assessment (DPIA) is a structured risk analysis required by Article 35 of GDPR before beginning processing that is likely to result in a high risk to the rights and freedoms of individuals. It is not a compliance checkbox — it is a decision-making tool that forces a structured examination of what data you are processing, what can go wrong, and what controls you are putting in place before deployment begins.

Claude deployments almost universally satisfy criterion 8 of the WP29 guidelines (use of innovative or novel technology), because generative AI that processes personal data falls squarely within the category of processing where the technology itself introduces risks that are not yet well understood by regulators or data subjects. This means that virtually any Claude feature touching personal data requires at minimum a DPO judgment about whether a full DPIA is required, and typically will require one.

Article 35(3) GDPR lists three categories that always require a DPIA: systematic, extensive profiling; large-scale processing of special category data; systematic monitoring of publicly accessible areas. Many Claude applications involving employee data, customer analytics, or health information fall into one or more of these mandatory categories without any borderline analysis needed.
The DPIA Trigger Decision Tree

attrs.alt: DPIA trigger decision tree showing the path from 'Does this process personal data?' through WP29 high-risk criteria to Mandatory DPIA, Borderline Assessment, or No DPIA required

content.text: Mapping WP29 Criteria to Claude Feature Patterns

The Article 29 Working Party (now the European Data Protection Board) published nine criteria for identifying high-risk processing. For Claude deployments, several criteria are commonly triggered simultaneously:
Criterion 1: Evaluation and Scoring
Triggered by:

any Claude feature that assesses individuals — credit risk scoring using financial data in context, employee performance analysis, candidate screening, fraud propensity scoring, customer churn prediction. The key question is not whether the output is called a 'score' but whether Claude is producing an inference about an individual that can be acted upon.
Criterion 2: Automated Decision-Making with Legal or Significant Effect
Triggered by:

Claude-powered decisions that are made without meaningful human review and that produce outcomes affecting legal status, financial access, employment, or service access. A loan approval system where Claude's recommendation is automatically implemented without a human credit officer reviewing the case is the paradigmatic example. If your system prompt instructs Claude to approve or deny, and that decision is executed automatically, this criterion is met.
Criterion 4: Sensitive Data
Triggered by:

any processing of special category data under Article 9 GDPR — health data (medical AI assistants, symptom checkers, clinical note summarisers), biometric data, genetic data, data revealing racial or ethnic origin, political opinions, religious beliefs, trade union membership, or sexual orientation. Processing financial data for vulnerable individuals or criminal offence data under Article 10 also triggers this criterion.
Criterion 5: Large Scale

The EDPB does not define a precise threshold for 'large scale'. Factors to consider: number of data subjects (GDPR Recital 91 gives examples of an entire city's residents, or a hospital's patients); volume of data; duration of processing; geographic extent. A Claude deployment serving more than 5,000–10,000 users with access to their personal data is commonly assessed as large scale by DPOs.
Criterion 8: Innovative Technology
Almost always triggered by Claude deployments.

The EDPB guidance explicitly lists AI-based profiling, neural network-based analysis, and large language model processing as examples. The rationale: novel technologies may have unknown side effects, and data subjects cannot reasonably anticipate how their data will be processed or what inferences will be drawn from it.
Building the Trigger Assessment Tool

import anthropic, json

client = anthropic.Anthropic()

WP29_CRITERIA = {
    "evaluation_scoring": "Does this feature evaluate, score, or rank individuals based on their behaviour, characteristics, or personal attributes?",
    "automated_decision_legal_effect": "Does this feature make or recommend decisions about individuals that have legal or similarly significant effects (employment, credit, insurance, service access) with limited human review?",
    "systematic_monitoring": "Does this feature systematically monitor individuals' behaviour, communications, or location — including online tracking?",
    "sensitive_data": "Does this feature process special category data (health, biometric, genetic, racial/ethnic, political, religious, sexual orientation) or financial data about vulnerable individuals?",
    "large_scale": "Will this feature process personal data of more than ~5,000 users, or process data continuously over an extended period?",
    "dataset_combination": "Does this feature combine or cross-reference datasets that individuals would not reasonably expect to be combined?",
    "vulnerable_subjects": "Does this feature process data about children, employees (in an employer-employee context), or other vulnerable groups where power imbalance affects meaningful consent?",
    "innovative_technology": "Does this feature use AI, machine learning, or other novel technology to process personal data in ways that may have unknown or unexpected effects?",
    "cross_border_transfer": "Does this feature transfer personal data outside the EEA, or to a country without an adequacy decision, without Standard Contractual Clauses or equivalent safeguards?"
}

def assess_dpia_trigger(feature_description: str) -> dict:
    """
    Assess whether a Claude feature requires a DPIA based on the WP29 9 criteria.
    Returns a structured assessment with per-criterion verdicts and overall recommendation.
    """
    criteria_str = json.dumps(WP29_CRITERIA, indent=2)
    prompt = f"""You are a GDPR data protection expert. Assess the following Claude feature description
against the nine WP29 high-risk processing criteria to determine whether a DPIA is required.

Feature description:
{feature_description}

Criteria to assess:
{criteria_str}

Respond with a JSON object containing:
- "criteria_results": an object where each key is a criterion name from the list above, with value:
  {{"triggered": true/false, "reasoning": "one sentence explaining why"}}
- "triggered_count": integer count of criteria triggered
- "dpia_required": "mandatory" | "borderline" | "not_required"
- "dpia_rationale": 2-3 sentences explaining the overall determination
- "dpo_consultation_required": true/false
- "highest_risk_criteria": list of the 2-3 most significant criteria that were triggered

Scoring: mandatory if >= 2 criteria triggered; borderline if exactly 1 triggered; not_required if 0.
Note: criterion 'innovative_technology' is almost always triggered for Claude features."""

    response = client.messages.create(
        model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001",
        max_tokens=1536,
        messages=[{"role": "user", "content": prompt}]
    )
    return json.loads(response.content[0].text)


feature = """
An AI-powered performance review assistant for HR managers. The manager provides the employee's
name and role, and the assistant analyses their 12 months of activity data (Jira tickets, code commits,
meeting attendance from calendar, Slack message volume) to generate a written performance summary
and a recommended rating (Exceeds / Meets / Below expectations). The recommended rating feeds
automatically into the annual bonus calculation system with no additional human review.
"""

result = assess_dpia_trigger(feature)
print(json.dumps(result, indent=2))
Mandatory vs. Discretionary DPIA Situations
Claude Feature Pattern
Mandatory DPIA Triggers
Notes
Employee performance / HR analytics
Criteria 1, 2, 7 (evaluation, automated decision, vulnerable subjects)
Employer-employee power imbalance means consent is rarely freely given; always DPIA mandatory
Medical / clinical AI assistant
Criteria 4, 5 (sensitive data at scale)
Any health data processing is Article 9 special category; DPIA mandatory regardless of scale
Customer credit / fraud scoring
Criteria 1, 2, 5 (evaluation, automated decision, large scale)
Financial decisions with significant effect; DPIA mandatory
Recruitment / candidate screening
Criteria 1, 2 (evaluation, automated decision)
Employment decisions have significant legal effect; DPIA mandatory
Internal knowledge base search (no personal data indexed)
Criterion 8 only (novel technology)
If the indexed content contains no personal data, borderline at most; DPO judgment
Customer support chatbot (no profiling)
Criterion 8 (novel technology)
Borderline; if no individual-level decisions, DPO may determine DPIA not required
Public-facing content generation (general audience)
Criterion 8 only
Low risk if no personal data in prompts; DPO judgment; DPIA likely not required
Documenting the Trigger Assessment

Even when the conclusion is that a DPIA is not required, the trigger assessment itself must be documented. Article 35(1) requires the controller to be able to demonstrate its reasoning. A trigger assessment document serves as evidence that the processing was evaluated before deployment and that the determination not to conduct a full DPIA was based on a principled analysis, not an oversight.
Trigger Assessment Record Template

DPIA TRIGGER ASSESSMENT
Feature: [Name of Claude feature or use case]
Assessment date: YYYY-MM-DD
Assessed by: [Name, role]
DPO review: [Name, date] | Not required (see rationale)

Personal data involved:
[List data categories: name, email, health records, location, behavioural data, etc.]
[Estimated number of data subjects and processing volume]

WP29 Criteria Assessment:
1. Evaluation/scoring: [Yes/No] — [rationale]
2. Automated decision (legal effect): [Yes/No] — [rationale]
3. Systematic monitoring: [Yes/No] — [rationale]
4. Sensitive data: [Yes/No] — [rationale]
5. Large scale: [Yes/No] — [rationale]
6. Dataset combination: [Yes/No] — [rationale]
7. Vulnerable subjects: [Yes/No] — [rationale]
8. Innovative technology: [Yes/No] — [rationale]
9. Cross-border transfer: [Yes/No] — [rationale]

Criteria triggered: [N] of 9

Determination:
☐ DPIA mandatory (≥ 2 criteria) — full DPIA must be completed before deployment
☐ Borderline (1 criterion) — DPO judgment required; see notes below
☐ DPIA not required (0 criteria) — document this record and proceed

DPO notes (if borderline):
[DPO rationale for requiring or waiving full DPIA]

Next steps:
[Link to DPIA if required | confirmation of waiver | deployment approval date]
Interaction with Anthropic's Data Processing Agreement
When you deploy Claude via the API, Anthropic acts as a data processor under GDPR Article 28. Your DPIA must account for this processing relationship. Key points:
Anthropic's DPA.
Anthropic publishes a Data Processing Agreement that addresses Article 28 requirements. Your DPIA should reference this agreement and confirm its terms are adequate for your processing purpose.
Data minimisation in prompts.

Your DPIA should assess whether the personal data sent to Claude's API in prompts and context is minimised to what is necessary. Including full PII in prompts when only anonymised data is needed is a risk that the DPIA should flag.
Retention and deletion.

Claude's API does not retain conversation data by default (subject to Anthropic's current privacy policy). Your DPIA should confirm the retention terms and assess whether any data is logged by your own infrastructure.
Cross-border transfer.

API calls to Anthropic's services involve data transfer to the US. Your DPIA should address the adequacy mechanism (Standard Contractual Clauses in Anthropic's DPA, or the EU-US Data Privacy Framework if applicable to Anthropic's certification).
DPIA Trigger Assessment in a Feature Delivery Pipeline

Most organisations that get DPIA compliance wrong do so not because they lack a process, but because the trigger assessment happens too late — after a feature is already in QA or about to go live, when changes are expensive and political pressure to ship is high. The correct integration point is the design phase, before engineering work begins.
Embedding Trigger Assessment in Agile Delivery
Stage
DPIA-Relevant Activity
Gate Condition
Product discovery
Product manager identifies whether the proposed feature processes personal data and completes a preliminary criteria checklist
No discovery proceeds to design without a preliminary data inventory
Design / architecture
Full WP29 criteria assessment completed; DPO consulted if borderline or mandatory
Features requiring DPIA cannot move to sprint planning until DPIA is initiated
Sprint planning
DPIA completion (if required) is added to the Definition of Done
Engineering team confirms DPIA status before story points are committed
Development
Data flows implemented according to DPIA risk mitigation commitments (minimisation, pseudonymisation, access controls)
Code review checklist includes DPIA compliance items
QA / staging
DPO confirms that DPIA mitigations are implemented as designed
Sign-off from DPO before staging environment receives production-class personal data
Launch
DPIA completed, signed, and filed; trigger assessment record archived
No launch without documented DPIA completion or documented non-requirement
The Ongoing Trigger Assessment Obligation
A DPIA is not a one-time event.
Article 35(11) GDPR requires that controllers review DPIAs when the processing changes in a way that may change the risk level. For Claude deployments, the following changes trigger a re-assessment:
Adding new data categories to the context window (e.g., expanding from anonymised queries to user-identified queries)
Changing the model version, where the new version has substantially different capabilities that alter the risk profile
Expanding the user population (e.g., from internal pilot to all customers globally)
Adding tool-call capabilities that give Claude access to systems holding personal data
Changing the decision-making pattern (e.g., from recommendation to automated decision)
Changing the data processor (e.g., switching from Anthropic's API to a self-hosted fine-tuned model)
Non-EU Jurisdictions and Equivalent Requirements

GDPR's DPIA framework is the most detailed, but equivalent requirements exist in other major jurisdictions. An enterprise deploying Claude globally needs to understand how these requirements interact.
Jurisdiction
Equivalent Requirement
Key Difference from GDPR DPIA
UK (UK GDPR)
Data Protection Impact Assessment — identical to EU GDPR Article 35
ICO publishes a specific AI guidance that adds algorithmic transparency obligations
Brazil (LGPD)
Relatório de Impacto à Proteção de Dados (RIPD) — Article 38
Required for processing likely to generate risks to data subjects; ANPD can request any RIPD at any time
California (CPRA)
Privacy Risk Assessment — required for businesses that process sensitive personal information or make decisions using automated means
Less prescriptive than GDPR; no mandatory 9-criteria framework; focuses on necessity and proportionality
Canada (Bill C-27 / AIDA)
Algorithmic Impact Assessment under AIDA; Privacy Impact Assessment under PIPEDA/C-27
AI-specific assessment required for 'high-impact AI systems'; overlaps with but is distinct from privacy impact assessment
Singapore (PDPA)
Data Protection Impact Assessment — recommended by PDPC for high-risk processing
Not legally mandatory under current PDPA; PDPC guidance strongly recommends it for AI systems
China (PIPL)
Personal Information Protection Impact Assessment — Article 55 requires it for sensitive data, automated decisions, cross-border transfers
Assessment must be kept for at least 3 years; PIPL adds requirement to report findings to relevant authority

When operating Claude globally, the GDPR DPIA framework is the most demanding in terms of documentation and DPO consultation requirements. A DPIA conducted under GDPR Article 35 standards generally satisfies the equivalent requirements in UK GDPR, LGPD, and PIPL, though jurisdiction-specific items (e.g., PIPL cross-border transfer rules) may require supplementary sections.
Practical DPIA Trigger Scenarios: Worked Examples
Scenario A: Internal Legal Document Summariser
Description:
A Claude-powered tool that allows lawyers to upload contracts and receive AI-generated summaries. Documents contain third-party party names, addresses, and financial terms.
Analysis:

Personal data is present (third-party individuals named in contracts). Criterion 8 (novel technology) is triggered. No systematic monitoring (criterion 3), no automated decisions with legal effect (criterion 2), no sensitive data in the Article 9 sense (criterion 4). Criterion 6 (dataset combination) may be triggered if contracts are cross-referenced against other databases. Likely 1–2 criteria triggered.
Determination:

Borderline to mandatory. DPO consultation required. If the tool stores contracts and enables cross-referencing, full DPIA required. If documents are processed transiently with no storage, borderline — DPO judgment.
Scenario B: AI-Powered Mental Health Support Chatbot
Description:
A Claude-based chatbot for employee mental health support. Employees describe their feelings and receive supportive responses. Conversations are logged for quality assurance.
Analysis:

Health data (criterion 4), vulnerable subjects (criterion 7 — employer-employee context with power imbalance), large scale if enterprise-wide (criterion 5), novel technology (criterion 8). Minimum 3–4 criteria triggered.
Determination:

DPIA mandatory. This is one of the clearest mandatory DPIA cases for a Claude deployment. The combination of sensitive health data, vulnerable subjects, and automated processing by an employer creates a very high-risk processing profile that requires a full DPIA before any pilot deployment.
Scenario C: Public Website FAQ Chatbot
Description:

A Claude-powered chatbot on a public-facing website that answers questions about a company's products and services. No user accounts, no login, no personal data collected beyond IP address via access logs.
Analysis:

IP address may constitute personal data under GDPR but is processed by the web server, not by Claude. The prompts sent to Claude contain only product questions with no personal identifiers. Criterion 8 (novel technology) is triggered. No other criteria are triggered if the system prompt and conversation context contain no personal data.
Determination:

Likely not required. Only criterion 8 is triggered; DPO review confirms that Claude processes no personal data in the context window. Trigger assessment record should document this finding and confirm the IP address access log processing is handled separately under an existing DPIA or lawful basis assessment.
Common Trigger Assessment Errors
Treating 'we anonymised it' as automatic disqualification.

Pseudonymised data (where re-identification is possible with additional data) is still personal data under GDPR. Many 'anonymised' datasets used with Claude contain enough quasi-identifiers to be re-identifiable. Confirm that anonymisation is genuine before using it to avoid a DPIA trigger.
Conflating data minimisation with non-processing.
Including only the user's first name in a prompt rather than their full name is data minimisation, not non-processing. Personal data is still being processed and the trigger assessment still applies.
Assessing only the data you send, not the data Claude infers.

If Claude infers health status from described symptoms, or infers political opinion from described news consumption patterns, those inferences may constitute processing of sensitive data even if you did not provide health or political data directly.
Completing the trigger assessment after development begins.
The trigger assessment must be completed in time for its findings to influence the design. A post-hoc assessment that rubber-stamps an already-built system is not a lawful DPIA process.
Assuming internal tools are exempt.

GDPR applies to processing of personal data about employees as well as customers. An internal HR tool is subject to the same DPIA requirements as a customer-facing product — and in some respects (power imbalance, mandatory participation) it carries higher risk.
Summary

DPIA trigger assessment for Claude features applies the nine WP29 high-risk processing criteria to the specific data flows, decision patterns, and subject populations of the proposed deployment. Because virtually all Claude features involving personal data satisfy criterion 8 (innovative technology), a single additional criterion triggers mandatory DPO consultation, and two or more criteria trigger a mandatory full DPIA before deployment. The trigger assessment must be documented even when a full DPIA is not required. Automated trigger assessment tools using Claude's own API can accelerate this process while maintaining consistency across a large portfolio of features.
